I kinda hate these movies because it never adresses that they have supernatural powers and pretends like they're just normal magicians, while obviously including the kind of magic that would never, ever, be feasible with a practical effect or trick.

I'd be fine with either, but the "magic" just being.. whatever, however, whenever, zero explanations takes me out of it.

Even superpowers usually have some sort of a limit or mechanic that you "get", real magic certainly does.. but this just makes this feel like there's absolutely no suspense when they can quite literally do whatever and nothing's explained.

"Close your eyes, count to ten, ta-dah, we're all in France!"

To me, it sounds like you haven’t watched the movies. They don’t act nor claim to have special powers, but rather are masters at deception. Our eyes lie to us all the time, and they prove that with even a slither of distraction, one of capable of changing an individuals narrative and giving the magician the upper hand. That’s what magic is about. I saw this movie last night and it was amazing. It is all perspective, deception, and timing that comes into it. Give it a chance before you speak ill of it.

​@arainymorning  Also they're just lying when saying nothing is explained. The France teleportation thing is not only based on a real Copperfield illusion, but very clearly explained within the film (most magic in these films are based on something real and clearly explained). Similarly, complaining about the rain scene from 2 is an obvious tell that someone hasn't seen them. This person is simply stupid, as are most people going into these. Which is odd, because those same people also claim only stupid people can enjoy these.

Loneliness

That night, after a fight with Ali, I decided to sleep in the other room. It was just me and Ali living in the house, and earlier he had promised to take me out somewhere. But instead, he made an excuse about a friend’s bachelor party and left without any explanation. I had even asked our maid, Noor, to stay until Ali returned, but she too left, saying her kids needed her.

Alone in the house, I started feeling scared. I thought maybe watching TV would help distract me. But just as I turned it on, there was a loud knock at the door. I quickly got up, my heart filled with a bit of hope—maybe Ali had come back. "Come inside already, how long will you take?" I said a bit angrily. But when I opened the door, my smile faded—it wasn’t Ali. Then suddenly, he appeared from somewhere. I started complaining to him.

I went to the other room, laid the bedding, and lay down without talking to him. I was still angry. He went to the bathroom to freshen up while I tried to sleep under the blanket. But then I heard a loud clattering sound, like utensils falling. I muttered in frustration, "I know he’s doing this on purpose so I’ll come help him!" Just as I put my head on the pillow, I heard another loud noise, like something was thrown in the kitchen. I was angry, but now a strange fear was creeping into my heart.

I decided to go and check. As I ran toward the kitchen, my heart was pounding, like something strange was about to happen. When I entered the kitchen, everything was in its place—nothing had moved. I quickly checked the room again, but no one was there. I checked the bathroom—also empty. My heartbeat grew faster, and I was drenched in sweat. Something was wrong, very wrong.

The thought crossed my mind to go outside and get help. As I walked toward the door, my phone started ringing—it was in the room. I gathered the courage to pick it up, and before I could say anything, it was Ali. "Don’t wait for me tonight. I’ll be late," he said. His words sent a chill through me. I went to open the door to leave.

Just as I opened the door and started stepping outside, a terrifying sound came from behind. I turned around—and my breath stopped. A tall, dark shadow was standing in the kitchen doorway, staring straight at me. There was a fire in its eyes that shook me to my core. I quickly shut the door and ran outside, screaming as loudly as I could for the neighbors. They rushed over and checked inside, but no one was there.

Even today, when the night grows quiet and deep, that terrifying shadow sometimes appears again. I still don’t know what it is. But it never leaves my mind—as if it’s following me, watching my every move.

so okay, clearly the movie's not about magician performers cleverly using ingenious stage tricks to steal a diamond, the movie's about characters who have actual, factual magic. the guy from the first movie is shown to swap places with Jesse Eisenberg in an instant, and Eisenberg's SURPRISED he's there there at all. 

so the movie isn't "Oceans 11 With Stage Magicians", it's more like "The Harry Potter Heist". not stagecraft but _witchcraft._ 

so okay, fine, they're actual witches and warlocks and that's just the world of the movie. so then why are they bothering to put on a public show for the villain? the trailer makes it clear they're in this for clear moral reasons, not for infamy. so if they want the diamond so badly, why don't they warp in at night and magically swap it when nobody's there to clumsily try and stop them??

Time was, some of the best scenes in movie history literally consisted of a few actors in a room and nothing more. Glengary Glen-Rose opening, Jaws USS Indianapolis story,  Godfather at the meeting with other mob bosses, Apocalypse Now mission briefing, the entire movie of 12 Angry Men, the list goes on and on. This series has always made me a little sad.  The idea of a bunch of professional magicians using their knowledge and skills to right wrongs (and steal stuff) is utterly awesome.  BUT the series decided to make them actual super heroes (with functional wizard powers), which I actually think makes the concept way more generic and gives the writers an easy "magic button" to get out of any situation.  There's never any real tension as a result.

These films are still a silly, fun time, but it's a waste of a truly exceptional concept.
